does: This paper conducts a comprehensive analysis of evaluation methodologies used in jailbreak attack research by surveying nearly 90 jailbreak studies published between May 2023 and April 2024. The authors develop a systematic taxonomy that categorizes different jailbreak evaluators, examining their strengths, weaknesses, and current adoption rates across the research community. They introduce JailbreakEval, an integrated toolkit that implements multiple evaluation approaches out-of-the-box, enabling researchers to assess the harmfulness of LLM responses generated under jailbreak attempts using different methodologies without building custom evaluation pipelines. The toolkit addresses the fragmentation in evaluation standards that currently exists across jailbreak research.
connects: This work builds on emerging research into jailbreak attacks and defenses for large language models, a subfield that has grown substantially as LLMs have become more widely deployed. It responds directly to the methodological fragmentation problem identified in the literature: while numerous papers propose jailbreak attacks and defenses, they employ inconsistent and often ad-hoc evaluation approaches, making comparison and cumulative progress difficult. The paper extends prior work on LLM safety and alignment by systematizing the evaluation component, which has received less structured attention than attack and defense mechanisms themselves. It connects to broader efforts to establish evaluation standards in AI safety and to frameworks for assessing model behavior against specified criteria.
enables: JailbreakEval enables more rigorous and comparable research on jailbreak attacks and defenses by providing researchers with standardized evaluation infrastructure. This toolkit supports broader adoption of consistent evaluation practices across the jailbreak research community, facilitating meta-analyses of attack and defense effectiveness. By making multiple evaluation methodologies accessible in one place, it allows researchers to understand how evaluation choice affects conclusions about attack success, potentially revealing discrepancies between different assessment approaches. The work points toward developing consensus evaluation standards for jailbreak research and could support the creation of benchmarks for assessing LLM robustness to adversarial misuse. It also enables more efficient resource allocation by helping researchers identify which evaluation methods best align with their goals while minimizing computational and financial costs.
